title: Trauma + EMDR Therapy
subtitle: You've been wearing the mask for so long, you're not sure what your actual face looks like anymore.

sections.title: The version of you that shows up at work? Totally fine. Competent. Together.
sections.body:
The version that exists when you're *alone*? Exhausted. Hypervigilant. Constantly bracing for the next thing to go wrong.

You've tried everything to fix it. Meditation apps that make you more anxious because you can't stop your brain long enough to meditate. Self-help books that make perfect sense when you read them, but somehow don't translate to your actual life. Therapy that helped... a little... but never quite got to the root of why you *still* feel this way.

sections.title: Here's what nobody tells you about trauma: it doesn't live in your thoughts. It lives in your nervous system.
sections.body:
That's why you can logically understand you're safe now, but your body still reacts like you're not. That's why you know the relationship should feel secure, but you keep waiting for the other shoe to drop. That's why you're exhausted all the time, even though nothing particularly exhausting happened today.

Your nervous system is stuck in a threat response that made sense at one point in your life, but now it's running the show when it shouldn't be.

sections.title: That's exactly what EMDR therapy targets.
sections.body:
EMDR (Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing) isn't talk therapy. It's not about rehashing your entire history or spending years unpacking every childhood memory. It's a neurobiological intervention that helps your brain actually \*process\* and \*release\* the stuck experiences your nervous system has been holding onto.

Here's how it works: When something overwhelming happens, your brain sometimes can't fully process it. The memory gets stored in a fragmented way, with all the emotions, body sensations, and beliefs from that moment still attached. So even though the event is over, your nervous system keeps responding as if it's still happening.

EMDR uses bilateral stimulation (usually tapping or vibrating buzzies, but can also be eye movements or audio tones) to help your brain reprocess those memories the way it was supposed to in the first place. The memory doesn't disappear, but the emotional charge and physical response that's been hijacking your life? Those start to fade.

sections.title: What that actually looks like in your life:
sections.body:
You stop getting triggered by things that used to send you spiraling. You can have difficult conversations without your heart racing or shutting down completely. You stop reacting to your partner like they're about to hurt you when logically, you know they're not. You finally feel present in your own body instead of constantly monitoring for danger.

You get to be the calm, grounded version of yourself you've been faking, except this time it's real.

sections.title: Do I have to share sensitive or upsetting details?
sections.body: In short, no. EMDR therapy allows you to desensitize and reprocess traumatic experience(s) without having to share in any explicit detail.

sections.title: How is EMDR different than talk therapy?
sections.body: EMDR is considered a bottom-up treatment approach, meaning we focus on helping you regulate and adjust your responses by tapping into your body’s nervous system. This is different than traditional talk therapy’s top-down approach which focuses on your thoughts.

sections.title: EMDR is particularly effective for:
items: Complex trauma and developmental trauma (the stuff that happened repeatedly, not just one big event)
Anxiety and panic that seems to come out of nowhere
Relationship patterns you can't seem to break (self-sabotage, attracting unavailable people, pushing away the good ones)
Physical symptoms tied to stress (chronic pain, digestive issues, tension)
Perfectionism and fear of failure that keeps you stuck
Hypervigilance and feeling constantly on edge

sections.title: KEEP GOING
sections.body:
I work with people who are exhausted from managing their nervous system's overreactions. People who are tired of pretending they're fine when their body is screaming otherwise. People who want to stop living in survival mode and actually experience what safety feels like.

If you've been searching for "EMDR therapy near me" or "trauma therapist Leesburg," you probably already know this is what you need. If you landed here because you Googled "why do I keep ruining my relationships" or "can't relax ever," then welcome. This is exactly where you need to be.
